summary: Gruber offers wide-ranging first impressions of Apple's just-announced iPad, framing it through his 'automatic transmission' metaphor: the iPad abstracts away the underlying computer just as automatics abstracted away gear shifting. He examines the new popover UI elements as a thoughtful evolution beyond iPhone's full-screen view switching, notes the surprising addition of hardware keyboard support (practical but unpolished), critiques iBooks typography as merely web-caliber rather than print-caliber, and observes that Apple's aggressive $499 starting price signals a land-grab strategy prioritizing unit sales over margins.
key_points: The iPad represents computing's 'automatic transmission' moment — abstracting away the file system and OS complexity for most users, while manual computers like Macs become a niche for experts
Popovers are a well-designed new UI paradigm that dramatically reduces full-screen view switching compared to iPhone apps, enabling split views and layered interfaces
Hardware keyboard support is a surprisingly practical but clearly unfinished addition — keyboards are limited to text input with no ability to navigate the interface
iBooks typography is disappointing: full-justified text without hyphenation creates ugly word-spacing gaps, falling short of the print-caliber quality expected from Apple
Apple's aggressive pricing strategy signals they prioritize market dominance and unit sales over per-unit margins in the mobile computing land grab
money_quotes: A computer running iPhone OS still has a hierarchical file system; the user just never sees it.
Manual computers, like the Mac and Windows PCs, will slowly shift from the standard to the niche, something of interest only to experts and enthusiasts and developers.
This is one of those aspects of the iPad UI that you just have to feel to get. It feels perfect.
1024 × 768 somehow seems very different on the iPad than on Mac OS — physically smaller but conceptually bigger.
The full-screen concept, without Mac-style overlapping draggable windows, leaves the iPad free to use as many pixels as possible for display content rather than UI chrome.
The on-screen iPad keyboard is not bad at all, for what it is, but it's exactly what you think — it's for pecking not typing.
It's decent web-caliber typography, not print-caliber typography.
The mobile computing landscape is in land-grab mode, and Apple is trying to stake out a long-term dominating position.
key_insight: The iPad represents computing's shift from manual to automatic — abstracting away the computer itself for most users, just as automatic transmissions abstracted away gear shifting for most drivers.
